votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic for character case swapping, string reversal, and the conditional logic based on the presence of letters, matching the Coq specification's behavior on concrete inputs.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including character classification, case swapping, string mapping, and the conditional logic in solve_spec.
The Python code correctly implements all helper functions and the main specification, matching the Coq logic for string manipulation, character checking, and the conditional logic in solve_spec.

The Python code correctly implements all the Coq definitions. ascii_code uses ord(), is_upper/is_lower check the correct ASCII ranges, swapcase_char correctly swaps case by adding/subtracting 32, string_map applies a function to each character, has_letter checks if any character is alphabetic, and string_rev reverses the string. The solve_spec function returns the same boolean judgment as the Coq Prop would evaluate to for any concrete inputs s and r.

The solve_spec function returns the same boolean judgment as the Coq Prop would evaluate to for any concrete inputs s and r.

Matches ASCII-based logic: upper/lower checks use 65–90/97–122, swapcase via ±32, map and letter detection align, and reverse auxiliary returns rev(s) + acc. solve_spec compares the same results.

Python mirrors Coq’s concrete behavior: ASCII range checks for upper/lower, swapcasing via +/-32, map and any for letter detection, and string reversal (rev s ++ acc) via slicing. For all ASCII inputs (Coq’s domain), results match.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: ASCII upper/lower checks via code ranges, swapcase by +/-32, map and has_letter behave the same, string_rev_aux returns rev(s) ++ acc, and solve_spec selects swapcase vs reverse accordingly. For all ASCII strings, results match.
